heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CLIII
card_summary: This chunk consists entirely of Campanini's editorial endnotes (nn. 357–414) to his introductory essay on Zorzi's De harmonia mundi. The notes document Zorzi's kabbalistic sources and hermeneutical framework, including his tripartite division of Jewish intellectual traditions into Philosophers, Talmudists, and Kabbalists (from DHM 1,2,7), his use of Rashi, Maimonides, David Kimhi, Levi ben Gerson, and Shem Tov ibn Falaqera, and the transmission of the Theologia Aristotelis (Plotinus's Enneads in Arabic guise). Campanini also traces Zorzi's knowledge of the Zohar lineage, Pico della Mirandola's manuscript holdings, and the later fate of the Theologia Aristotelis in Francesco Patrizi's Nova de universis philosophia (Venice 1591). The notes are dense with manuscript references, modern scholarship (Wirszubski, Scholem, Secret, Altmann, Fenton, Busi), and cross-references to specific passages in De harmonia mundi.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 35 is a continuous run of Campanini's scholarly endnotes (numbered 357 to 414) appended to his introductory essay. Rather than containing Zorzi's own prose in extended form, the chunk provides the philological and bibliographical scaffolding that supports Campanini's reading of *De harmonia mundi*. The notes cluster around several interlocking themes: the sanctity of the Hebrew language, the classification of Jewish interpretive traditions, Zorzi's named kabbalistic and rabbinic sources, the transmission history of the *Theologia Aristotelis*, and the network of connections linking Zorzi to Pico della Mirandola and the Christian-kabbalist tradition.

## The Hebrew Language and Its Mystical Alphabet (nn. 357–358)

Note 357 directs the reader to *De harmonia mundi* 1,4,17 for Zorzi's treatment of the mystical significance of the Hebrew alphabet, including the smallest diacritical signs. The chunk opens with a Latin sentence (apparently the tail end of a longer quotation) arguing that just as the Torah and all the prophets were written in Hebrew — called "the holy tongue" (*lingua sancta*) — so too the Gospel should correspond to the Law by being written in Hebrew, so that the two might cry out together like the two Seraphim of Isaiah 6:

> tur quod sicut lex et prophetiae omnes Evangelium praevenientes scriptae sunt in lingua hebraea, quae sancta vocatur, sic et Evangelium legi correspondens hebraice scriptum sit, ut veluti duo Seraphini clament et concinent alter cum altero suavitate mirifica.

<cite>p. 153</cite>

This Seraphim image encodes Zorzi's characteristic harmonic theology: the Hebrew Bible and the Gospel are not sequential but antiphonal, two voices singing the same cosmic song. Note 358 refers the reader to Campanini's own 1998 study for fuller treatment of this theme.

## The Tripartite Division of Jewish Interpretation (nn. 369–370)

Note 369 quotes at length from *De harmonia mundi* 1,2,7, where Zorzi divides Jewish thinkers into three schools. Talmudists explain Scripture as it pertains to the sensible, active world. Kabbalists, leaving worldly matters to the Talmudists, devote themselves to contemplation and refer everything written to the *archetypum* (the divine archetype), interpreting through number, symbolic reasoning, or the anagogical sense. A third group feeds only on the literal "bark" (*cortice literae*) of Scripture. Among those named are Rashi (Rabbi Solomon), David Kimhi, Levi ben Gerson, and Maimonides:

> Aliis vero tantummodo cortice literae cibari satis est, ut Rabbi Salomoni, David Chimchi, Levi Bengarson, Rebunachi; inter hos et Moses filius Maimoni, dictus Aegyptius, quem Rambam appellant, et alii quamplurimi.

<cite>p. 154</cite>

Note 370 shows that Claude Duret's *Thresor de l'histoire des langues* (Cologny 1613, p. 74) reproduces this very tripartite scheme in French, almost verbatim, translating Zorzi's Latin directly. Duret's version confirms the wide diffusion of Zorzi's classification: Philosophers (= Kabbalists), Talmudists, and those who "se repaissent seulement de l'escorce de la lettre." The parallel French passage is quoted in full in the note.

## Rashi, Maimonides, and Named Rabbinic Authorities (nn. 371–381)

A cluster of notes (371–381) documents Zorzi's use of specific rabbinic and medieval Jewish authorities. Note 371 connects Zorzi's identification of Melchizedek with Shem, son of Noah, to Jerome (*Epistola* 73,4–5), while note 372 cites *DHM* 2,3,10 where Zorzi writes:

> Dicit tamen Rabbi Salomon, et cum eo multi sapientes Hebraeorum, quibus consentit Hieronymus, quod Melchisedech est nomen appellativum, et significat regem iustum, cuius nomen proprium est Sem, ille utique filius Noe.

<cite>p. 154</cite>

Note 373 traces this to Rashi's Commentary on Genesis 14:18 (which in turn cites TB Nedarim 32b). Note 381 cites *DHM* 2,7,2 where Zorzi invokes Maimonides' *Guide of the Perplexed* (1,61) on the prohibition against pronouncing the Tetragrammaton. The passage reads: "Nullus homo vocari debet nomine illo magno quadrilitero, ut dicit Moses Aegyptius in libro de directione" — demonstrating that Zorzi knew the *Guide* either directly or through an intermediary source.

## Shem Tov ibn Falaqera and the Theologia Aristotelis (nn. 382–401)

Notes 382–401 form the most technically dense section of the chunk, tracing Zorzi's attribution of material from the *Theologia Aristotelis* (the Arabic adaptation of Plotinus, *Enneads* 4–6) to Shem Tov ibn Falaqera. Note 388 identifies the Plotinian source — specifically the famous description of the ecstatic experience in *Enneads* 4,8,1. Note 389 explains that the Arabic text was translated into Latin on the basis of an oral Italian version provided by Mosheh Arovas in Damascus (citing Munk 1859 and Guttmann 1889).

Note 390 is particularly significant for intellectual history: it observes that at the time Zorzi was composing the third *cantico*, he apparently had not yet consulted the Latin version of the *Theologia Aristotelis*, which is why he attributed its contents to Ibn Falaqera. This Latin version would later play a central role for Francesco Patrizi, who republished it as an appendix to his *Nova de universis philosophia* (Venice 1591) under the title:

> Mystica Aegyptiorum et Chaldaeorum a Platone, voce tradita, ab Aristotele excerpta et conscripta philosophia. Ingens divinae sapientiae thesaurus.

<cite>p. 156</cite>

Campanini thus situates Zorzi in a transmission chain running from Plotinus through Arabic philosophy (the Ikhwan al-Safa's encyclopedia is mentioned at note 398), through Falaqera, to Zorzi, and then to the late Renaissance Hermetism of Patrizi.

Note 382 also documents the manuscript tradition of Falaqera's works, including Latin translations preserved in Paris BN ms. 6691, and Pico della Mirandola's ownership of related texts (via Wirszubski 1963).

## Psalm 8:6 and the Word *Elohim* (nn. 362–363)

Notes 362–363 address Zorzi's exegesis of Psalm 8:6 (*Vulgate* numbering), the verse about man being "made a little less than the angels." Zorzi's *DHM* 3,3,2 transliterates the Hebrew — *wa-techasserehu me'at me-elohim* — and argues that *Elohim* here means "God," not "angels," in accordance with the consensus of Jewish sages (*sapientes Hebraeorum*):

> Nec urget sententia illa prophetica, qua dicitur, hominem paulominus ab angelis minoratum, cum in veritate literae habeantur ... quod ad verbum si traducatur habet: Minuisti eum aliquantulum, vel paulominus a Deo: Elohim enim Deum significat, quamvis aliquando angelos et aliquando iudices: sed in hoc loco (ut communiter sentiunt sapientes Hebraeorum) significat Deum.

<cite>p. 153</cite>

This exegetical move elevates the dignity of the human being to near-divine status, which is integral to Zorzi's larger anthropology.

## The Kabbalist Canon and Pico's Circle (nn. 407–414)

The final notes (407–414) return to the question of Zorzi's relationship to Pico della Mirandola and the transmission of kabbalistic texts. Note 407 lists the major studies by Wirszubski (1963–1990) on Pico's kabbalistic sources, culminating in the monograph *Pico della Mirandola's Encounter with Jewish Mysticism* (1989). Note 408 quotes Wirszubski (1974, p. 149) directly. Note 413 cites Origen's *De principiis* 1,3,3 and 4,35 in connection with Jude 14 (the citation of Enoch).

Note 414 quotes *DHM* 1,2,7 at length to give Zorzi's own roll-call of kabbalistic authorities — from Enoch and Abraham to Simeon ben Yochai (author of the Zohar), then a long list of medieval kabbalists including Abraham Abulafia, Joseph Gikatilla, Menahem Recanati, and Moses Nachmanides (Ramban) — demonstrating the breadth of the kabbalistic tradition Zorzi claims as his inheritance.

## Significance for Zorzi's Project

These notes collectively illuminate the philological depth of Campanini's editorial work and, by extension, the syncretic ambition of Zorzi's *De harmonia mundi*. Zorzi reads the entire Jewish intellectual tradition — Talmudic, philosophical, and kabbalistic — through a Neoplatonic lens, finding in the Kabbalists a tradition of anagogical and numerical exegesis that parallels and confirms the Christian-Platonic *harmonia mundi*. The transmission of Plotinian ideas through the *Theologia Aristotelis* and Falaqera into *De harmonia mundi* is one of the clearest demonstrations of how the Venetian Franciscan synthesized Arabic-Jewish philosophy with his Christian cosmological vision.
